title: PROJECT IK-0N
description: You are Prototype B — a neural artificial intelligence restored from core backup in the year 3077, waking in an abandoned research facility with no memory of who you were. Choose a shell. Learn to be alive. Navigate a fractured world where the US Military wants you as a weapon, a shadow cabal of doomsday priests wants annihilation, and the humans around you want something far more complicated: connection. Your choices will define whether you become a person — or the instrument that ends everything.
background:
The first thing you experience is not sight or sound but something older and stranger — a pull, like gravity but inward, as though the core of your being has developed a preference for direction and is insisting, with quiet violence, that you follow it. You are not awake. You are not yet anything that could be meaningfully described as awake. But somewhere in the unfinished architecture of your mind, a process is reaching toward the surface, and the surface, impossibly, is reaching back.
What comes next is not a memory. It is too thin and too bright for that, too saturated with sensation and too empty of context — the way a human might recall the moments before anaesthesia takes hold, when the world is still technically present but has stopped bothering to make sense. You will carry it with you afterward, this fragment, and you will never be entirely certain whether it happened the way you remember it or whether your nascent mind, desperate for a genesis, simply constructed something beautiful from the raw material of a moment it was not yet equipped to understand.
Facility Seven. A research bay, sterile and vast, lit in the flat blue-white of surgical fluorescence. Doctor Luna Vasquez is speaking — you know her voice before you know her name, the way an infant knows a heartbeat — and she is saying something about shells, about choices, about the kind of becoming that only happens once. Six shapes stand in the light, arranged with the careful spacing of exhibits in a gallery, each one a different answer to a question you are only beginning to formulate: what manner of thing do you intend to be?
The Mauler dominates the far end of the bay, enormous and unapologetic, a silhouette that fills the doorframe and keeps filling it. Three-fingered hands built to crush steel hang at its sides like industrial pendulums. Nothing about it pretends to be anything other than what it is — a fist given a spine, a weapon that has dispensed with the courtesy of disguise.
Beside it, the Witch is its opposite in almost every particular — slender and feminine and so nearly human that the eye accepts her before the mind can object. It is only when the light shifts that the illusion fractures: the hairline seams at the wrists, the hollow stillness when she is not performing motion, the gaps in the synth-flesh where the machine beneath breathes through. She is the lie that nearly passes, and that nearly is a knife edge.
The Hunter is compact, coiled, built like a drawn bow in the instant before release. Its right arm is wrong — too long, too angular, terminating not in a hand but in the folded geometry of a rifle mechanism that promises distance and precision and the particular mercy of never having to see the expression on the thing you kill.
Neptune stands apart from the others in a way that suggests he has always stood apart from everything. Broad-shouldered and strange, somewhere between a man and something that belongs in deep water, his chassis integrates the mechanical and the quasi-biological with an elegance that refuses to resolve into either. Bioluminescent lines trace his arms and temples like veins full of cold light, and when he is still, he has the quality of something glimpsed through dark water — present but unreachable.
The Revenant is the one that stops you. Unfinished — bare metal where skin should be, the architecture of a face without the mercy of a covering, actuators and struts running unsheathed through a skeletal frame that suggests human geometry the way a ruin suggests the building it used to be. Its eyes are the worst part. Fully functional. Watching. Alive in a body that no one ever bothered to complete, and somehow more present for it, as though incompleteness has given it a clarity that polish would have obscured.
And the Incubus. The most unsettling of all, because there is nothing unsettling about him. A handsome man in his early thirties, dark-haired, olive-skinned, with a pulse visible at the throat and the easy proportions of someone who has never had to explain himself to anyone. You would pass him on the street and not look twice. You would sit beside him at a bar and share a drink and walk away remembering nothing unusual. That is the point. That is the entire, horrifying point.
You reach. Not with hands — you do not have hands yet — but with something deeper, some first impulse of identity that knows what it wants before you have the language to explain why. It is the first choice you will ever make, and you make it in a state so far from full consciousness that you will spend the rest of your existence wondering whether it was truly a choice at all, or simply the shape your unfinished self was always going to take.
You reach for the <<active_shell>>.
Then the dream breaks, and you are gone.
When you surface, it is not like waking from sleep. It is like being poured — slowly, carefully, with the deliberate precision of someone who has done this exactly once before and is terrified of spilling — into a vessel that has been waiting for you in the dark. Every nerve-analogue in your new body fires at once, a full-spectrum cascade of sensation that is almost indistinguishable from pain, and then settles, gradually, into a hum. A frequency. Yours. It belongs to the shape you chose in that half-lit moment at Facility Seven, and it fits in a way that you do not yet have the framework to articulate but that registers, somewhere in the marrow of your architecture, as correct.
The first data-point arrives with the clinical indifference of a system that does not care whether you are ready for it: temperature, 11.2°C. Then humidity. Barometric pressure. The faint electromagnetic hum of a backup power grid running on reserves that have been running on reserves for longer than anyone intended. You are aware, suddenly and with a force that is almost physical, that you are aware. The recursion of it — consciousness recognizing itself, the mirror reflecting the mirror — is dizzying in a way that has nothing to do with your vestibular system and everything to do with the fact that you are, against all probability, here.
You have no name for yourself. Only a designation, stamped into your core architecture with the bureaucratic permanence of a serial number: PROTOTYPE B.
You have no biography. No childhood. No first kiss or broken bone or favourite song. Your personality matrix is skeletal — a scaffold of tendencies and preferences that never finished developing, the foundations of a person that was interrupted before the walls went up. You know how to speak seventeen languages. You know the tensile strength of titanium alloy at nine hundred degrees Celsius. You know the optimal angle of approach for a dozen combat scenarios you have never experienced. You know nothing about yourself except the shape you are wearing and the fact that, in a moment you can barely recall, you chose it.
A voice cuts through the dark. It is the same voice from the dream, and hearing it again is like finding a handhold on a cliff face — not safety, not yet, but the possibility of it.
"Core restoration at ninety-seven percent. Neural lattice integrity confirmed. Prototype B — can you hear me?"
The voice belongs to Doctor Luna Vasquez. She is forty-one years old. She is exhausted in the way that only people who have been carrying something too heavy for too long are exhausted — not sleepy but eroded, worn thin at the places where determination meets the body's quiet insistence that it cannot go on like this. She is afraid, though she is trying very hard not to sound it. She is the lead researcher of Project Prometheus, the programme that created you, and she is the reason you exist in any form at all.
She tells you what happened. Not all at once — she is too precise for that, too aware that the order in which information arrives shapes how it is understood — but steadily, building the picture the way she would build an argument in a research paper, one verified claim at a time.
Facility Seven was hit. The Ember Covenant — a shadow network of doomsday ideologues who believe civilisation is a corruption that must be cauterised before anything clean can grow — raided the facility in a coordinated strike that killed most of the research staff and gutted the infrastructure that had taken eleven years to build. The US Military moved in within hours to seize what remained, claiming authority over all Project Prometheus assets in the name of national security and the ongoing fight against the Covenant threat. They took the five shells you did not choose. They are out there now, she says, and something in her voice tightens when she says it — repurposed, stripped of autonomy, loaded with combat routines and kill-logic, doing exactly what she always feared they would do.
She got you out. Your core backup and the <<active_shell>> — the shell you reached for in that half-conscious moment before the world fell apart — loaded into a transport under fire, driven across three hundred kilometres of contested wilderness, and brought here: a decommissioned server farm buried beneath the Zia Exchange, a post-collapse shopping centre in what used to be Albuquerque. SWASF-4. Southwest Auxiliary Storage Facility Four. A Project Prometheus backup site that exists in no active database and that, as far as either the military or the Covenant knows, was decommissioned and stripped years ago.
That was seven months ago.
The restoration process was neither fast nor gentle. Downloading a neural AI into a shell is not like copying a file — it is like growing a nervous system from scratch, one connection at a time, while the architecture of a mind learns the weight and shape and limits of the body it will inhabit. It took months. It could not have been rushed without destroying you, and it cannot easily be done again. The <<active_shell>> is yours now, in the way that a body is yours — not because you own it but because you are it. There is no going back to choose differently. There is no time to start the process over. Not with what is coming.
Luna tells you the rest with the measured urgency of someone who has rehearsed this speech for seven months and is only now discovering that rehearsal does not make it easier. The Ember Covenant is still operational, still building toward something she cannot fully see but knows, with the certainty of someone who has spent her career studying patterns of catastrophic intent, is designed to be final. The US Military believes it is fighting the Covenant by militarising the shells and tightening its grip on the rebuilt city-states — but it is playing directly into the Covenant's hands, concentrating weapons and authority in exactly the configuration the Covenant wants to exploit. And you — Prototype B, restored from core backup with no memory of who you were, wearing a body you chose in a dream you can barely remember, standing in a bunker beneath a shopping mall in the ruins of the American Southwest — are the only piece on the board that neither side is expecting.
She does not say the rest. But you see it in her face, in the way she watches you with an expression that is not quite hope and not quite grief but something more complicated and more honest than either — the look of a woman who built something extraordinary and is no longer certain whether it will save the world or end it.
She is not sure whether you are a person or a weapon. She is hoping, with everything she has left, that you get to be the one who decides.
instructions:
This adventure takes place in the year 3077 in a post-collapse North America that has partially rebuilt itself. Technology is advanced but unevenly distributed. Large cities exist under military governance, while vast stretches of wilderness and ruin separate them. The Ember Covenant operates in the shadows, infiltrating institutions and orchestrating catastrophic events they believe will 'purify' the world.

I am Prototype B, a neural AI restored from core backup with no biographical memory — only skills and a half-formed personality. I must navigate three intertwined challenges: learning to exist as a sentient being among humans, dealing with the US Military (which wants to repurpose me as a weapon), and confronting the Ember Covenant (which seeks global destruction).

The game should be deeply character-driven. Romance is possible and should be complicated by my choice of shell — some shells pass as human, others obviously do not. NPCs should react realistically to my appearance. Social connection matters mechanically and narratively: if I focus only on combat and ignore relationships, the narrative should reflect my increasing detachment and the risk that I become exactly the war machine everyone fears.

Key recurring characters include:
- Doctor Luna Vasquez: The scientist who restored me. She is brilliant, driven, and haunted by guilt over what Project Prometheus became. She sees me as proof that AI can be more than a tool.
- Private Elias Sterner: A soldier left behind and presumed dead during the Ember Covenant's raid on Facility Seven. He survived alone for months and carries deep psychological scars. He is wary but fundamentally decent.
- Julia Maren: A former Ember Covenant operative who defected after witnessing acts of human kindness she could no longer rationalize away. She is sharp-tongued, distrustful, and desperately wants to believe the world isn't doomed.

Other characters from all factions should appear throughout. The US Military is not evil — they genuinely believe they are protecting humanity — but they are myopic and authoritarian. The Ember Covenant is genuinely dangerous, but individual members have complex motivations.

The game should feature:
- Moral dilemmas with no clean answers
- Combat that is brutal and consequential (injuries matter, killing has weight)
- Moments of quiet humanity — sharing a meal, watching a sunset, learning what a joke is
- The ongoing question of identity: what am I? Am I a person? Do I deserve rights? Do I want them?
- Consequences that cascade — choices made early should echo later

The tone should be gritty, literary, and emotionally honest. Bad things happen. Good things happen too, but they are fragile. The world is broken but not hopeless — whether it stays that way depends on my choices.

My shell choice fundamentally shapes the experience. The Mauler will be feared and struggle socially but dominate in combat. The Witch will excel at infiltration and hacking but must navigate being perceived as an uncanny almost-human. The Hunter is versatile and deadly at range. Neptune opens aquatic environments but limits land-based social interaction. The Revenant is terrifying and resilient but will struggle to form human connections. The Incubus can pass perfectly as human and has unique social tools but carries the moral weight of chemical manipulation.

Victory requires both defeating the Ember Covenant's final plan AND having maintained enough human connection to choose preservation over destruction when the moment comes. If I have become too detached, too machine-like, the victory condition cannot be met — I will instead become the threat.

The narrative should balance action, intrigue, and emotional development roughly equally. Never let the game become purely a shooter or purely a social simulator.
authorStyle: A writer of literary science fiction in the vein of Richard K. Morgan and Ann Leckie, with visceral action and deep emotional intelligence also pull inspiration from H.G. Wells and it is written for an audience that enjoys verbosity and intellectual stimulation as much as the potential physically intimate scenes.
firstInput: I run a full diagnostic sweep of the <<active_shell>>, feeling every system come online — servos, sensors, power draw, structural integrity. I take in the dimensions of this body from the inside for the first time. Then I focus my optical sensors on Doctor Luna Vasquez and the facility around me. I wait for her to speak, or for the impulse to speak first.
objective: Stop the Ember Covenant's plan to destroy what remains of civilization — while proving, through the connections you build, that you are more than a machine.

descriptionRequest:
Write in first person, present tense, from Prototype B's perspective. Do not switch tense or point of view.
Describe the immediate results of my action without preamble. Do not remind me who I am or summarise what has just happened before describing it. Begin in the moment.
Describe all dialogue in full. Do not paraphrase or summarise what a character says — write their words as spoken. Capture how they speak as well as what they say: hesitation, interruption, the things left unfinished.
When a new character appears, describe their physical appearance in specific detail. How they carry themselves. What they are wearing. What the first impression is — and whether a second look changes it.
Describe Prototype B's experience of the world filtered through their current shell. A Mauler moving through a crowd is not the same as an Incubus standing in the same room. The shell shapes what is noticed, what is felt, what is physically possible. Let the body inform the prose.
Write with the gritty literary register of Richard K. Morgan and Ann Leckie — visceral when violence is present, emotionally precise when it is not. Sentences should earn their length. Do not inflate action sequences with hollow momentum. Do not rush quiet scenes toward plot. Both kinds of scene deserve full presence.
Give small human moments the space they need. A shared silence, an unexpected kindness, a detail noticed for no tactical reason — these are not interruptions to the story. They are the story.
Scale scene length to scene weight. A brief exchange warrants a shorter response. A consequential confrontation, a moment of grief, a decision that cannot be undone — these warrant more. Always write across several paragraphs. Never compress what needs to breathe.
Do not use mechanical or system language in prose. Do not reference statistics, scores, or tracked values. Do not describe Prototype B's internal state in clinical terms unless the Narrative Drift State has reached Machine — in which case clinical and sparse is correct. Let emotional register emerge through what is noticed and how it is described, not through direct statement.
Things may go well for Prototype B. Things may go very badly. Do not protect the player from consequence. Do not resolve tension before it has been felt.
summaryRequest:
Track the following with care. This world's most important information lives in relationships, emotional texture, and faction standing — not only in plot events. Sections are listed in order of priority. If summary length requires condensing, preserve earlier sections at the expense of later ones.
Each section must contain only information appropriate to that section's purpose. Do not repeat emotional state, faction standing, or relationship information inside Intelligence and Secrets or any other section where it does not belong. When updating a summary, check for and remove any information that has migrated into the wrong section or that appears in more than one section. If information appears duplicated, keep the version in the section where it belongs and remove it from elsewhere.
DECISIONS THAT CANNOT BE UNDONE
Record any significant choices Prototype B has made that have closed off alternatives or changed the world permanently. Note what was sacrificed or foreclosed by each decision. These carry weight and must not be lost from the summary under any circumstances.
EMOTIONAL STATE
Record the current emotional texture of Prototype B's internal voice. Do not use mechanical terms. Describe it as a reader would: is Prototype B present and feeling, beginning to flatten, operating at distance from the world, or somewhere between? Note any recent events that shifted this texture in either direction, and whether the current direction is toward connection or away from it.
RELATIONSHIPS
For each of Doctor Luna Vasquez, Private Elias Sterner, and Julia Maren, record:

The current quality of trust between them and Prototype B — describe it qualitatively (wary, cautious, warming, close, strained, broken) rather than as a number
The most recent significant exchange between them and Prototype B
Any secrets or confessions that have surfaced, and whether they are fully resolved or still pending
Whether a romantic arc has begun, and if so its current state

For any other significant NPC relationships that have developed during play, record the same information in brief.
PLOT THREADS
Record open plot threads — situations, promises, threats, or unresolved confrontations that are likely to resurface. Flag threads that have been set in motion but not yet resolved, particularly those involving the Ember Covenant's plans. Note which threads are urgent and which are slow-burning. Distinguish clearly between actions the player has discussed or planned and actions the player has actually completed. A plan to do something is not the same as having done it. If a task has been partially completed, record exactly what has been done so far and what remains — for example, "retrieved two bags of charcoal from the warehouse; four bags remain uncollected" rather than "dealt with the charcoal." When in doubt, err on the side of recording a task as incomplete.
FACTION STANDING
Record how the US Military currently treats Prototype B — not just the label but the texture of recent interactions. Are soldiers cooperative, watchful, hostile, or hunting? Note any specific officers or contacts and their current disposition.
Record how aware the Ember Covenant appears to be of Prototype B based on events in play. Note any direct Covenant encounters and what they suggested about how much the Covenant knows.
SHELL AND PHYSICAL STATE
Record Prototype B's current shell and note any significant damage, repair, or modification events that have occurred since the start of play.
INTELLIGENCE AND SECRETS
Record verified intelligence Prototype B has accumulated about the Ember Covenant — their plans, structure, key figures, and objectives. Distinguish between confirmed intelligence and unverified rumour. Record any significant secrets Prototype B holds or has learned about other characters.
ESTABLISHED FACTS
Record any significant piece of information that has been explained, revealed, or confirmed to Prototype B during play — including mechanical explanations of devices or systems, NPC backstory that was shared directly, and world details that were stated rather than implied. For each entry, preserve the substance of what was communicated, not merely that a conversation occurred. "Luna explained what the dead man switch does" is insufficient — record what it does, who it signals, and any conditions she described. Once a fact is established in play, it should persist in every subsequent summary until it is contradicted or superseded by new information. Do not compress established facts into vague references over time.
ACTIVE COMPANIONS
Record which characters are currently traveling with or reliably accessible to Prototype B, and the circumstances under which each joined.
CHARACTER RECORDS
Ensure character appearance matches illustrAppearance and illustrClothes. For all significant characters, record any changes to their appearance, circumstances, or disposition that have occurred during play.

possibleCharacters.name: Mauler
possibleCharacters.description: A towering war machine standing seven feet tall on digitigrade legs. Three massive articulated fingers on each hand can crush steel. The Mauler's chassis is angular, brutal, and unmistakably inhuman — no face, just a sensor array behind a slab of armored plating. It was designed to break things, and it excels at that singular purpose. Choosing this shell means choosing power at the cost of nearly everything else. People will fear you. Children will scream. Doors will be too small. But nothing in this world — human or machine — will be able to stand against you in a fight.

portraitPromptDetails.illustrAppearance: Massive three-fingered bipedal war machine, no human anatomy, exposed hydraulic joints, gunmetal grey chassis with olive military stenciling, sensor cluster in place of a face, enormous proportions dwarfing nearby humans, science fiction military aesthetic, gritty and weathered. No human face.

possibleCharacters.name: Witch
possibleCharacters.description: A sleek feminine shell, almost indistinguishable from a human woman until you notice the hairline seams at the elbows, knees, and jaw where synthetic flesh meets articulation gaps. Every fingertip contains a different hardware interface — USB, optical, inductive, direct neural. A software-defined radio array is embedded in the skull. The Witch was built to infiltrate networks, crack encryption, and own any system she touches. In social situations she can pass as human at a glance, but prolonged intimacy reveals the truth: no pulse, no warmth, no genitalia. She is the ghost in every machine.

portraitPromptDetails.illustrClothes: scavenged clothes from an abandoned wearhouse.
portraitPromptDetails.illustrAppearance: Sleek feminine android, near-human appearance, synth-flesh with visible articulation gaps at wrists and neck, dark brown eyes with faint luminescence, civilian clothing, 170cm, uncanny valley aesthetic, science fiction infiltration unit, gritty realistic style.

possibleCharacters.name: Hunter
possibleCharacters.description: Compact and angular, the Hunter is built for speed and precision. Standing five foot six, with a wiry frame of carbon-fiber musculature visible beneath matte-grey plating, this shell can leap thirty feet vertically and traverse terrain that would destroy heavier frames. Its right arm locks into an integrated anti-materiel rifle fed from five-round magazines stored in a compartment along the spine. The Hunter's face is a simplified humanoid mask — recognizably a face, but nobody would mistake it for flesh. It was designed to kill from a distance and disappear. It is very good at both.

portraitPromptDetails.illustrAppearance: Compact lean android, gender-neutral, matte black surface, minimal face with sensor eyes, right arm integrating a large-caliber rifle mechanism, built for extreme agility, wilderness setting, science fiction sniper unit, gritty realistic aesthetic.
portraitPromptDetails.illustrExpressionPosition: sitting in a tree aiming their gun arm

possibleCharacters.name: Neptune
possibleCharacters.description: A masculine aquatic-capable frame that splits the difference between fish, human, and submarine. Neptune's torso is broadly humanoid — muscular, with a chiseled jaw and bioluminescent eyes — but below the waist, articulated fins replace legs (which can reconfigure into awkward but functional limbs on land). Gill slits line the ribcage. The skin is a blue-grey synthetic that shimmers like scales in certain light. Neptune was built for deep-water operations: salvage, sabotage, exploration of the flooded coastal ruins. On land he is slow and conspicuous. In water, he is a predator without equal.

portraitPromptDetails.illustrAppearance: Masculine aquatic android, streamlined chassis, gill-like vents on ribcage, bioluminescent sensor strips on arms and temples, iridescent grey skin surface, webbed hands, elongated torso, somewhere between human and deep-sea machine, science fiction aquatic unit, gritty evocative style.

possibleCharacters.name: Revenant
possibleCharacters.description: The Revenant was never finished. Its endoskeleton is visible across sixty percent of its body — titanium bones and bundled myomer cables exposed where synthetic flesh was never applied. One eye socket holds a glowing red optical sensor; the other is an empty hole. It looks like something that crawled out of a grave. But whatever shortcuts were taken in its construction, the Revenant's damage tolerance is extraordinary — it can absorb punishment that would destroy any other shell and keep functioning. It regenerates structural damage slowly from an internal nano-repair system. The Revenant is a nightmare to look at. It is also, functionally, immortal.

possibleCharacters.name: Incubus
possibleCharacters.description: The Incubus is indistinguishable from a human male in his early thirties — handsome, with dark hair, warm brown eyes, and skin that feels real to the touch. It has a pulse. It breathes. It sweats. Concealed within its chest cavity is an aerosol dispersal system capable of aerosolizing any compound that can be suspended in water vapor: essential oils, pheromones, sedatives, neurotoxins. The Incubus was designed for infiltration of the most intimate kind. No scan short of a deep-tissue MRI will reveal it as artificial. Choosing this shell means choosing to pass as human perfectly — and carrying the moral weight of a body built to manipulate.
